text: This is our single anastomosis with duodenal ileostomy and sleeve gastrectomy otherwise known as CDS presentation. Hello, I'm Dr. Daniel Cottom and we've received many requests from our patients to have a full video of our CDS also known as our lube duodenal switch procedure. performed and put out on the internet.
Here we're going to, with the following video, show you how we do the looped abdominal switch in our daily practice, and this is true for both Dr. Medlin and I. I hope you enjoy the video.

The surgeon locates the ileocecal valve, where the colon meets the small bowel, and traces the small bowel backward to identify the site for the duodenum-ileostomy connection. This site is marked by tacking it to a piece of fat for easy identification during the subsequent steps of the operation.

The surgeon uses a cauterizing device to take down blood vessels connecting the arteries and fat to the stomach, sealing and cutting them to maintain a dry field. The stomach is dissected from the top down to the pylorus, and the duodenum is being dissected out at the bottom part of the stomach.

The surgeon addresses filmy adhesions posteriorly to access the duodenum. A window is created through the fat layer near the pylorus, marking the transition from the stomach to the duodenum. A standard bariatric clamp is placed inside the abdomen to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon uses a gastrointestinal anastomosis (GIA) stapler to excise the stomach, placing it next to a standard bariatric clamp. The stapler cuts and staples simultaneously, creating a straight staple line to prevent sleeve strictures.

The surgeon completes the sleeve gastrectomy and uses a liver retractor to move the liver aside for better access. A GIA stapler is positioned beyond the pylorus to cut across the duodenum. Reinforcement from the stapler device is removed, followed by the tacking stitch. The proximal duodenum is then attached to the distal loop limb, identified as the ileum.

The surgeon uses absorbable sutures to connect the cut edge of the duodenum on the right to the small bowel loop limb on the left, ensuring the connection is free of tension. The sutures are placed close to the fat layer while penetrating the small bowel tissue.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to create openings on either side of the duodenum, forming an enterostomy without significant bleeding. The posterior layer is then sutured together with absorbable sutures, ensuring a secure connection between the posterior halves.

The surgeon uses an endo stitch to hand sew a running stitch along the anterior portion of the duodenal opening, visible on the right side, to close the opening. The stitch is tied off to secure the closure.
The surgeon places an anti-obstruction stitch on the loop channel to ensure food travels down the correct side, preventing potential symptoms from incorrect passage.

The surgeon places a second anti-obstruction stitch to ensure there is no tension at the connection points. The stomach and small bowel are inflated with air to check for leaks, with no bubbles observed, indicating a secure connection.

After completing the air leak test, the surgeon dilates the abdominal wall and uses a large clamp to extract the distensible stomach through a small incision. A diagram illustrates the SADI-S procedure, showing the sleeve gastrectomy and the small bowel connection.

Our average operative time is approximately seventy minutes and people who have this done usually stay one day in the hospital or just an overnight stay in the surgical center. Thank you for paying attention to us. Have a good day.
